title: Tension Pneumothorax
oneLiner: Life-threatening accumulation of air in pleural space causing mediastinal shift and cardiovascular collapse; typically traumatic or iatrogenic, requires immediate decompression.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive haemothorax** – the “dark twin” of tension: hypotension + absent breath sounds but with dullness to percussion (not hyperresonance) and no tracheal deviation. The single finding: **chest drain yields >1500 mL blood** or continuing bleeding.
- **Pericardial tamponade** – hypotension + distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds (Beck’s triad). The single finding: **pulsus paradoxus** and **echo confirms pericardial fluid**.
- **Massive pulmonary embolism** – hypotension + raised JVP + hypoxia but **breath sounds are normal** (no unilateral absence) and ECG shows S1Q3T3 or right strain.
- **Ruptured oesophagus / tension gastrothorax** – if recent vomiting or endoscopy + left-sided chest findings. Single finding: **air in mediastinum or pleural space with food particles** on decompression.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset severe dyspnoea (often out of proportion)
  - Pleuritic chest pain (ipsilateral)
  - Feeling of impending doom / agitation
- **Key exam findings (classic triad):**
  - Ipsilateral **absent breath sounds** and **hyperresonance** to percussion
  - **Tracheal deviation** away from the affected side
  - **Distended neck veins** (raised JVP) + **hypotension** (late sign)
- **Other:** tachycardia, cyanosis, contralateral tracheal tug, reduced chest expansion on affected side
- **In ventilated patients:** sudden rise in peak airway pressures, hypotension, desaturation
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Simple pneumothorax** → no tension physiology (no hypotension, no tracheal deviation, JVP normal)
- **Haemothorax** → dullness to percussion, not hyperresonant; blood on aspiration
- **Pericardial tamponade** → pulsus paradoxus, Kussmaul’s sign, echo diagnostic
- **Massive PE** → bilateral breath sounds (or normal), raised JVP, ECG changes
- **Ruptured diaphragm (tension gastrothorax)** → stomach sounds in chest, history of trauma
- **Splitter:** **Immediate needle decompression** – if a rush of air is heard on needle insertion, tension pneumothorax is confirmed and simultaneously treated (bifurcates tension from its mimics)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Immediate clinical diagnosis** – do not delay imaging if patient unstable
  - **Needle decompression** is both diagnostic and therapeutic (see Management)
  - Bedside ultrasound (if available): “lung point” absent, “lung sliding” absent, “stratosphere sign”
- **Bloods:**
  - **ABG** – respiratory acidosis, hypoxia, hypercapnia (if ventilated)
  - FBC, U&E, clotting (if trauma or pre-procedure)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **Chest X-ray (PA)** – but ONLY if patient stable enough (rarely – most tension requires immediate decompression). Signs: deep sulcus sign, mediastinal shift away, ipsilateral lung collapse, hyperlucent hemithorax
  - **Second-line:** CT chest (if diagnosis uncertain or after decompression)
- **Never-miss test:** **Clinical assessment + needle decompression** – the most dangerous error is waiting for a CXR while the patient remains in tension. If suspicion high, **decompress first, image later**.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Oxygen:** high-flow 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask (if not ventilated)
  - **Needle decompression:**
    - **Site:** 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (or 5th intercostal space, midaxillary line – preferred for larger body habitus)
    - Use a large-bore (14–16G) cannula attached to a 3-way tap or flutter valve
    - Listen for rush of air; leave cannula in place secured with tape
  - **Definitive chest drain insertion** (intercostal drain, typically 24–28 Fr in adults for air)
    - Insert in “safe triangle” – 5th intercostal space, midaxillary line
    - Connect to underwater seal (no suction initially)
    - Confirm position with CXR after procedure
  - If needle decompression fails to improve physiology → **repeat decompression** or consider **other side / tension gastrothorax**
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Maintain chest drain on underwater seal + low-pressure suction (e.g. -5 to -10 cmH₂O) if air leak persists
  - Monitor for re-expansion pulmonary oedema (rare) – if hypotension/respiratory distress after drain insertion, call senior
  - Encourage coughing / deep breathing to aid lung expansion
  - Regular drain observation: swinging (respiratory variation in water column) indicates patent drain; continuous bubbling indicates air leak
  - Pain management: paracetamol + NSAIDs (if no contraindication) + stepwise opioid PRN
  - **Daily CXR** to assess lung re-expansion and drain position
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Once lung fully expanded and air leak stopped → clamp drain for 4–6 hours, repeat CXR; if no recurrence → remove drain
  - Consider thoracic surgery referral if persistent air leak >7 days or recurrent tension
  - Advise patient: avoid smoking, scuba diving, flying until fully resolved (typically 2–4 weeks after drain removal)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s or 0.9% NaCl) – rate as per BP; avoid aggressive boluses unless hypotension persists after decompression
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) once drain in situ and no bleeding risk (check local policy)
  - **Diet/NBM:** keep NBM until chest drain inserted under local anaesthetic? Usually nil-by-mouth if surgical drain insertion planned, otherwise clear fluids allowed
  - **Observation frequency:** baseline NEWS2, then q15min for first hour after decompression, then q1h until stable, then q4h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Analgesia* – Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS (max 4 g/day) + Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO TDS (if no renal impairment, asthma, or GI bleeding) + Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV PRN for severe pain (with antiemetic e.g. cyclizine 50 mg IV)
  - *Antibiotic prophylaxis during chest drain insertion* – [check local policy – often single dose of co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV or cefuroxime 750 mg IV if allergic]
- **Common alternatives:** Oxycodone 5–10 mg PO PRN (if morphine intolerance)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** NSAIDs in AKI, asthma, GI bleeding; opiates in respiratory depression
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** NSAIDs avoid in eGFR <30; morphine dose reduce in renal impairment (accumulation of active metabolite)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Not applicable for tension pneumothorax itself. However, **administration of IV fluids without first relieving tension** can worsen cardiac failure once decompression occurs; the key is **decompress first, then fluid resuscitate if needed**.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (unilateral, sudden hypoxia after drain insertion – treat with oxygen, sit up, diuretics only if fluid overload)
  - Infection (empyema) – fever, purulent pleural fluid; treat with antibiotics + drain
  - Drain malposition (intrafissure, intraparenchymal, mediastinal) – CXR ± CT to confirm
  - Haemothorax (if drain damages intercostal vessels)
- **Chronic:**
  - Persistent air leak (>7 days) – may need surgical pleurodesis
  - Recurrent tension pneumothorax – consider definitive management (thoracoscopy, chemical pleurodesis, or pleurectomy)
  - Fibrothorax/pleural thickening (rare with prompt drainage)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Excellent** if recognised and treated within minutes: mortality <1%
- **Untreated:** mortality approaches 100% due to obstructive shock / cardiac arrest
- **Recurrence rate:** up to 30% in spontaneous pneumothorax (if tension was first presentation); lower if underlying cause (trauma/iatrogenic) resolves
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always decompress clinically first** – never wait for a CXR in an unstable patient with tension signs. A positive “rush of air” confirms the diagnosis and treats it.
- **Use the 5th intercostal space midaxillary line for needle decompression** in obese patients – the 2nd ICS midclavicular is often too high or blocked by fat.
- **After needle decompression, have a chest drain ready** – the cannula can kink or clog; definitive drainage is required. Secure the cannula with tape until the drain is in.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not wait for CXR or CT** to confirm tension pneumothorax if the patient is hypotensive and has unilateral absent breath sounds – this wastes critical time.
- **Do not remove the decompression needle** before inserting the chest drain – the tension can reaccumulate rapidly.
- **Do not use a small-bore chest drain (<20 Fr)** for tension pneumothorax because it may not adequately evacuate air or cope with a large air leak; a 24–28 Fr tube is standard.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** sudden worsening dyspnoea, chest pain, or new unilateral absence of breath sounds (suggests recurrence of tension)
- **Return if:** fever, purulent sputum, or redness/swelling around drain site (infection)
- **Return if:** drain falls out or becomes disconnected – occlude site with sterile gauze and come back immediately
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic in 2–4 weeks for CXR + symptom review; if recurrent, refer to thoracic surgery
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU immediately if:**
  - Hypotension not improving after correct needle + drain decompression
  - Signs of re-tension (recurrent hypotension, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation)
  - NEWS2 ≥ 7 (or any single parameter red: RR >30, SpO₂ <85%, HR >140, SBP <90)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (indicates shock) despite drainage
  - GCS drop <13 or severe agitation/confusion
- **Call crash team if:** patient becomes pulseless (pulseless electrical activity/PEA – consider tension as cause of arrest, immediate bilateral needle decompression + chest drain)

rawMarkdown:
## Tension Pneumothorax

**One-liner:** Life-threatening accumulation of air in pleural space causing mediastinal shift and cardiovascular collapse; typically traumatic or iatrogenic, requires immediate decompression.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive haemothorax** – the “dark twin” of tension: hypotension + absent breath sounds but with dullness to percussion (not hyperresonance) and no tracheal deviation. The single finding: **chest drain yields >1500 mL blood** or continuing bleeding.
- **Pericardial tamponade** – hypotension + distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds (Beck’s triad). The single finding: **pulsus paradoxus** and **echo confirms pericardial fluid**.
- **Massive pulmonary embolism** – hypotension + raised JVP + hypoxia but **breath sounds are normal** (no unilateral absence) and ECG shows S1Q3T3 or right strain.
- **Ruptured oesophagus / tension gastrothorax** – if recent vomiting or endoscopy + left-sided chest findings. Single finding: **air in mediastinum or pleural space with food particles** on decompression.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset severe dyspnoea (often out of proportion)
  - Pleuritic chest pain (ipsilateral)
  - Feeling of impending doom / agitation
- **Key exam findings (classic triad):**
  - Ipsilateral **absent breath sounds** and **hyperresonance** to percussion
  - **Tracheal deviation** away from the affected side
  - **Distended neck veins** (raised JVP) + **hypotension** (late sign)
- **Other:** tachycardia, cyanosis, contralateral tracheal tug, reduced chest expansion on affected side
- **In ventilated patients:** sudden rise in peak airway pressures, hypotension, desaturation

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Simple pneumothorax** → no tension physiology (no hypotension, no tracheal deviation, JVP normal)
- **Haemothorax** → dullness to percussion, not hyperresonant; blood on aspiration
- **Pericardial tamponade** → pulsus paradoxus, Kussmaul’s sign, echo diagnostic
- **Massive PE** → bilateral breath sounds (or normal), raised JVP, ECG changes
- **Ruptured diaphragm (tension gastrothorax)** → stomach sounds in chest, history of trauma
- **Splitter:** **Immediate needle decompression** – if a rush of air is heard on needle insertion, tension pneumothorax is confirmed and simultaneously treated (bifurcates tension from its mimics)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Immediate clinical diagnosis** – do not delay imaging if patient unstable
  - **Needle decompression** is both diagnostic and therapeutic (see Management)
  - Bedside ultrasound (if available): “lung point” absent, “lung sliding” absent, “stratosphere sign”
- **Bloods:**
  - **ABG** – respiratory acidosis, hypoxia, hypercapnia (if ventilated)
  - FBC, U&E, clotting (if trauma or pre-procedure)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **Chest X-ray (PA)** – but ONLY if patient stable enough (rarely – most tension requires immediate decompression). Signs: deep sulcus sign, mediastinal shift away, ipsilateral lung collapse, hyperlucent hemithorax
  - **Second-line:** CT chest (if diagnosis uncertain or after decompression)
- **Never-miss test:** **Clinical assessment + needle decompression** – the most dangerous error is waiting for a CXR while the patient remains in tension. If suspicion high, **decompress first, image later**.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Oxygen:** high-flow 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask (if not ventilated)
  - **Needle decompression:**
    - **Site:** 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (or 5th intercostal space, midaxillary line – preferred for larger body habitus)
    - Use a large-bore (14–16G) cannula attached to a 3-way tap or flutter valve
    - Listen for rush of air; leave cannula in place secured with tape
  - **Definitive chest drain insertion** (intercostal drain, typically 24–28 Fr in adults for air)
    - Insert in “safe triangle” – 5th intercostal space, midaxillary line
    - Connect to underwater seal (no suction initially)
    - Confirm position with CXR after procedure
  - If needle decompression fails to improve physiology → **repeat decompression** or consider **other side / tension gastrothorax**
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Maintain chest drain on underwater seal + low-pressure suction (e.g. -5 to -10 cmH₂O) if air leak persists
  - Monitor for re-expansion pulmonary oedema (rare) – if hypotension/respiratory distress after drain insertion, call senior
  - Encourage coughing / deep breathing to aid lung expansion
  - Regular drain observation: swinging (respiratory variation in water column) indicates patent drain; continuous bubbling indicates air leak
  - Pain management: paracetamol + NSAIDs (if no contraindication) + stepwise opioid PRN
  - **Daily CXR** to assess lung re-expansion and drain position
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Once lung fully expanded and air leak stopped → clamp drain for 4–6 hours, repeat CXR; if no recurrence → remove drain
  - Consider thoracic surgery referral if persistent air leak >7 days or recurrent tension
  - Advise patient: avoid smoking, scuba diving, flying until fully resolved (typically 2–4 weeks after drain removal)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s or 0.9% NaCl) – rate as per BP; avoid aggressive boluses unless hypotension persists after decompression
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) once drain in situ and no bleeding risk (check local policy)
  - **Diet/NBM:** keep NBM until chest drain inserted under local anaesthetic? Usually nil-by-mouth if surgical drain insertion planned, otherwise clear fluids allowed
  - **Observation frequency:** baseline NEWS2, then q15min for first hour after decompression, then q1h until stable, then q4h

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Analgesia* – Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS (max 4 g/day) + Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO TDS (if no renal impairment, asthma, or GI bleeding) + Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV PRN for severe pain (with antiemetic e.g. cyclizine 50 mg IV)
  - *Antibiotic prophylaxis during chest drain insertion* – [check local policy – often single dose of co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV or cefuroxime 750 mg IV if allergic]
- **Common alternatives:** Oxycodone 5–10 mg PO PRN (if morphine intolerance)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** NSAIDs in AKI, asthma, GI bleeding; opiates in respiratory depression
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** NSAIDs avoid in eGFR <30; morphine dose reduce in renal impairment (accumulation of active metabolite)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Not applicable for tension pneumothorax itself. However, **administration of IV fluids without first relieving tension** can worsen cardiac failure once decompression occurs; the key is **decompress first, then fluid resuscitate if needed**.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (unilateral, sudden hypoxia after drain insertion – treat with oxygen, sit up, diuretics only if fluid overload)
  - Infection (empyema) – fever, purulent pleural fluid; treat with antibiotics + drain
  - Drain malposition (intrafissure, intraparenchymal, mediastinal) – CXR ± CT to confirm
  - Haemothorax (if drain damages intercostal vessels)
- **Chronic:**
  - Persistent air leak (>7 days) – may need surgical pleurodesis
  - Recurrent tension pneumothorax – consider definitive management (thoracoscopy, chemical pleurodesis, or pleurectomy)
  - Fibrothorax/pleural thickening (rare with prompt drainage)

### Prognosis
- **Excellent** if recognised and treated within minutes: mortality <1%
- **Untreated:** mortality approaches 100% due to obstructive shock / cardiac arrest
- **Recurrence rate:** up to 30% in spontaneous pneumothorax (if tension was first presentation); lower if underlying cause (trauma/iatrogenic) resolves

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always decompress clinically first** – never wait for a CXR in an unstable patient with tension signs. A positive “rush of air” confirms the diagnosis and treats it.
- **Use the 5th intercostal space midaxillary line for needle decompression** in obese patients – the 2nd ICS midclavicular is often too high or blocked by fat.
- **After needle decompression, have a chest drain ready** – the cannula can kink or clog; definitive drainage is required. Secure the cannula with tape until the drain is in.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not wait for CXR or CT** to confirm tension pneumothorax if the patient is hypotensive and has unilateral absent breath sounds – this wastes critical time.
- **Do not remove the decompression needle** before inserting the chest drain – the tension can reaccumulate rapidly.
- **Do not use a small-bore chest drain (<20 Fr)** for tension pneumothorax because it may not adequately evacuate air or cope with a large air leak; a 24–28 Fr tube is standard.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** sudden worsening dyspnoea, chest pain, or new unilateral absence of breath sounds (suggests recurrence of tension)
- **Return if:** fever, purulent sputum, or redness/swelling around drain site (infection)
- **Return if:** drain falls out or becomes disconnected – occlude site with sterile gauze and come back immediately
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic in 2–4 weeks for CXR + symptom review; if recurrent, refer to thoracic surgery

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU immediately if:**
  - Hypotension not improving after correct needle + drain decompression
  - Signs of re-tension (recurrent hypotension, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation)
  - NEWS2 ≥ 7 (or any single parameter red: RR >30, SpO₂ <85%, HR >140, SBP <90)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (indicates shock) despite drainage
  - GCS drop <13 or severe agitation/confusion
- **Call crash team if:** patient becomes pulseless (pulseless electrical activity/PEA – consider tension as cause of arrest, immediate bilateral needle decompression + chest drain)
